conventional_view: Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude: (1) the "exempt surplus land" declaration was likely wrong — the 25-year covenant term appears to fall short of the Surplus Land Act's durational requirements for affordable-housing exemptions, and the 18/18 low/moderate mix likely doesn't satisfy the narrow 100%-affordable exemption category, so HCD's NOV has real merit; the fix is a corrective process (extend covenants, run the NOA/negotiation period retroactively or restart it) to cure the defect and minimize/negotiate down the 30% penalty, not to unwind the whole DDA. (2) The $1 conveyance is not an unconstitutional gift because the affordable units plus public plaza constitute legally sufficient consideration under the public-purpose doctrine — courts give heavy deference to a legislative body's public-benefit findings in below-market affordable-housing dispositions (see, e.g., the general rule from cases upholding below-cost transfers for public purposes). (3) The five-year local preference carries genuine disparate-impact litigation exposure given the city's demographics, but is a common redevelopment/housing tool that can likely be preserved through redesign (narrower share, displacement/anti-displacement rationale, shorter or eliminated residency requirement, sunset clause) rather than abandoned entirely.
disfavored_position: The opposing pole holds: (a) the Surplus Land Act violation is not a curable technicality but voids the exempt-surplus-land basis for the entire disposition, requiring the city to restart the statutory NOA/negotiation process from scratch and exposing it to the full 30% penalty rather than a negotiated reduction; (b) the $1 conveyance is an actual unconstitutional gift of public funds under article XVI, section 6, with the public-purpose doctrine not supplying adequate consideration for a transfer of this magnitude to a private, for-profit developer; and (c) the five-year residency preference is independently unlawful — not merely risky — under FHA/FEHA disparate-impact doctrine and the AFFH duty, and cannot be saved by redesign short of its elimination or replacement with a mechanism untethered to local tenure.
